petitioner_background: The petitioner is a Chinese musician who plays the Gu Zheng (a plucked string instrument) and the Chinese Hammered Dulcimer (a trapezoidal-soundboard instrument). She sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. She has been residing in the United States since September 2000, and the petition was filed on May 17, 2003. Her career credentials largely stem from performances, awards, and activities in China during the 1990s.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted seven items: (1) Certificate from the Chinese Folk Music Association stating she 'won First Prize in the 10th Anniversary Folk Music Competition' (1998); (2) Certificate from the Chinese Musician Association and Chinese Folk Music Association stating she 'won the Second Award in the Chinese Harp Solo Performance of The 3rd Anniversary of the National Folk Music Performance' (1996); (3) Certificate from the Committee of Chinese Folk Musical Instrument of Chinese Folk Music Association stating she 'won the First Award in the Chinese harp performance in the 1st Anniversary of Fragrant of Peach and Prune, Teenagers Folk Music Concert' (1994); (4) Certificate from the Chinese Musician Association recognizing her as an 'outstanding national instrumental music educationist' (1998); (5) Certificate from the Chinese Music Concert Committee honoring her 'for her great contribution in the 2nd Chinese Music Concert' (1990); (6) Certificate from the Beijing Cultural Bureau presented to the Beijing Quju Opera Troupe for winning 'the First Award in the 86-89 Year of New Dramas competition Performance in Beijing City' (1989); (7) Student Award from the Chinese Music Institution (1982).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence of media publicity surrounding the petitioner's awards or evidence showing significant national/international recognition. The record contained no documentation from print media or from officials of the awarding entities establishing the awards as nationally recognized. For item 5, there was no evidence the certificate was a recognized award for excellence rather than mere acknowledgment of participation. Item 6 was characterized as a local (not national) award and was a group award, making the petitioner's individual contribution difficult to ascertain. Item 7 (student award) was deemed institutional in scope with experienced professionals excluded from consideration. The AAO also noted the absence of significant awards after 1998, indicating the petitioner had not sustained whatever acclaim she may have earned in China during the 1990s. The petitioner failed to provide official comprehensive competition results showing her ranking against other competitors.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of membership in the Chinese Folk Orchestral Association, the Chinese Folk Music Association, and the Chinese Musician Association. The petitioner submitted two undated and unsigned letters bearing seals of the Chinese Musician Association and Chinese Folk Orchestral Association. The Chinese Musician Association letter stated that applicants 'must have outstanding achievement in the art performing filed [sic] and high performing technique' and must provide 'at least two recommendation letters from two directors of the association.' The Chinese Folk Orchestral Association letter stated standards included: qualifying for the professional post, being a first level musical instrument performer, great contribution in the area of musical performing, and two recommendation letters from members.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no bylaws or official comprehensive membership requirements for these associations. The two submitted letters were undated, unsigned, and lacked any address, phone number, or contact information, making them of limited probative value. Neither letter specifically identified what constitutes 'outstanding achievement' or a 'great contribution in the area of musical performing,' nor established that membership applicants are evaluated by recognized national or international music experts. The AAO emphasized that membership requirements based on employment, experience, or recommendations by colleagues do not satisfy the criterion, and members must be selected at the national or international level. There was no substantive evidence that admission required outstanding achievement or that the petitioner was evaluated by national/international experts.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Articles appearing in Chinese-language newspapers published in New York and New Jersey, including World Journal, Singtao Daily, and Duowei Times.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this local media coverage was not evidence of national acclaim. There was no evidence that these newspapers have substantial national readership beyond Chinese-language readership in New York and New Jersey. Because the overwhelming majority of the U.S. population does not read or comprehend Chinese, articles in such publications were not shown to constitute 'major media.' The majority of articles were accompanied by incomplete or summary translations, failing to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires a full English translation certified as complete and accurate. In several instances, there was no indication the petitioner was the primary subject of the published material. The AAO noted that even with nationally-circulated newspapers, placement matters—an article distributed only in a specific county cannot spread reputation beyond it.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Two brief letters (five sentences or less) from July 1997 inviting the petitioner to serve as a judge at the '3rd Anniversary of the Chinese National Orchestral Music Festival.' A certificate dated May 1998 allegedly from the Chinese Musician Association stating the petitioner 'is employed as a judge of Classic Chinese Musical Instrument Inspection Committee.' Two unsigned letters bearing the seal of the Chinese Musician Association (exhibits 23 and 24) stating she was 'qualified for the judge position' and 'appointed as the professional judge' of the Classic Chinese Musical Instrument Inspection Committee. In response to the RFE, she submitted another unsigned letter dated September 2003 from the Chinese Musician Association stating she was 'appointed as judge of National Folk Music Panel,' and an unsigned letter dated September 2003 from the 'Art Department of Culture Bureau of the People's Republic of China' inviting her to be a judge in the 'Chinese Art Anniversary.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence the petitioner actually accepted the 1997 invitation to serve as a judge, nor any information regarding the expertise level of individuals to be evaluated (novice, amateur, or professional). There was no published material or national publicity surrounding her involvement in the festival. The record included no evidence of her actual activities as a judge for the Classic Chinese Musical Instrument Inspection Committee—no information regarding her duties, events judged, names of individuals evaluated, or their expertise level. The two invitation letters submitted in response to the RFE came into existence after the petition's filing date, and a petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. The letters also lacked address, phone number, or any contact information. The AAO emphasized that 'an invitation is not tantamount to participation,' and the regulation requires evidence of actual participation as a judge. Subsequent to her 2000 entry to the United States, there was no evidence she had actually served as a performing arts judge.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of authorship of one publication in what is alleged to be a series entitled 'Guide Book For Youth Learning Music,' and an article entitled 'The Performing Art of Yang Qin' in Huayue Music.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence of the field's reaction to these published works, nor any indication they are widely viewed as significantly influential. There was no evidence these publications enjoy substantial national or international readership.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's various musical performances.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this criterion more appropriate for visual artists (such as sculptors and painters) rather than performing artists. Virtually every musician 'displays' her work by performing in front of an audience, and in the performing arts, acclaim is generally not established by the mere act of appearing in public, but rather by attracting a substantial audience. The regulations establish separate criteria for performing arts. The petitioner's performances were deemed far more relevant to the 'commercial successes in the performing arts' criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted that she 'played leading roles in more than one hundred shows.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no indication that a 'show' constitutes an 'organization or establishment' for purposes of this criterion. It had not been shown that the petitioner's shows have earned a distinguished reputation at the national or international level. The evidence was inadequate to demonstrate that the petitioner performed in a leading or critical role for a distinguished organization, or that her involvement earned her sustained national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: An August 20, 2003 letter bearing the seal of the Beijing Qu Opera Troupe stating the petitioner earns a monthly salary of 'RMB 6000 Yuan' with potential to earn an additional 'RMB 1000 Yuan per performance.' Claims that she received '20 thousands [sic] RMB Yuan as a reward' from the Culture Bureau of Beijing City and '250 thousands [sic] RMB Yuan per year' in royalties from the sale of two recording compilations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the Beijing Qu Opera Troupe letter lacked address, phone number, or other contact information. The record included no financial documentation (such as payroll records or income tax forms) showing the petitioner's actual earnings for any period prior to the petition's filing date. A petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. The petitioner failed to provide adequate documentation of compensation prior to May 2003. The plain wording of the criterion requires evidence of high salary 'in relation to others in the field,' but the petitioner offered no basis for comparison. Subsequent to her 2000 U.S. arrival, there was no indication she earned compensation placing her among the highest paid musicians in the U.S.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Program booklets, correspondence, and photographs relating to performances. Evidence that her music was included in a compilation cassette entitled 'Urban Background Music' and on a compact disc entitled 'An Autumn Moony Night in the Hun Palace.' Reference to a certification from the 'Guang Zhou Audio-Video Press' indicating her music sold 'more than twelve thousands [sic] volumes.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that while the evidence showed performances took place, it did not establish commercial success. The plain wording of the criterion requires evidence of commercial success in the form of 'sales' or 'receipts.' There was no evidence the petitioner's performances drew record crowds, were regular sell-outs, or resulted in greater audiences than similar performances without her. The record did not include a copy of the Guang Zhou Audio-Video Press certification. The petitioner was not the only musician on the compilations, making her contribution to sales volume difficult to ascertain. It had not been shown that 12,000 copies sold is substantial given the immense size of China's population. There was no evidence her recordings generated greater sales than most other professionals in her field at the national or international level.

outcome_reasoning: The petitioner failed to demonstrate that she meets at least three of the regulatory criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. Beyond the regulatory criteria, the AAO considered letters of support, which indicated the petitioner is a talented Gu Zheng performer but fell short of demonstrating sustained national or international acclaim in the United States or China. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished herself as a musician to such an extent that she achieved sustained national/international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of her field. The evidence was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field at a national or international level. The AAO also emphasized that given the more than 32 months between her U.S. arrival (September 2000) and the petition's filing (May 2003), it was reasonable to expect her to have earned national acclaim in the United States during that time, which she had not. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner per section 291 of the Act, and she did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural issues: (1) Numerous submitted letters were undated and/or unsigned and bore only association seals without any address, phone number, or contact information, rendering them of limited probative value and not independently verifiable. (2) Foreign-language documents were submitted with incomplete or summary translations rather than full certified English translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). (3) Two invitation letters to judge were dated September 2003, after the May 17, 2003 filing date, and thus could not establish eligibility at time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. No explicit fraud finding was made.
